report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Illya, thanks for taking the time to complete this. Your answers paint a clear and interconnected picture, and there's a lot we can work with here. Let's walk through the key patterns.

### A Thyroid Under Pressure

The combination of always feeling cold, brain fog, hair thinning, brittle nails, early evening crashes, and a metabolism that feels slow all point in one direction: your thyroid may not be producing or converting enough active hormone. You've never had your thyroid tested, which means this has likely gone unexamined. Standard testing often only checks TSH, which can miss the real issue. The conversion of inactive T4 into active T3 (which happens mainly in the liver) is where dysfunction often hides, and this is heavily influenced by nutrient status, particularly [selenium and zinc, which are commonly low in plant-based diets](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10459941/). Iodine availability is another concern, as [vegetarian and vegan diets pose a recognized risk for iodine deficiency](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11556367/), a rate-limiting element for thyroid hormone production.

### Your Gut and Thyroid Are Talking to Each Other

Your constipation and bloating aren't separate from your thyroid picture. They're part of it. Thyroid hormones directly regulate gut motility, digestive enzyme secretion, and stomach acid production. When thyroid function is low, [the entire GI tract slows down](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/physiology/articles/10.3389/fphys.2024.1389113/full), food lingers, bacteria ferment it, and gas and bloating follow. The relationship runs both directions, too. A sluggish gut allows bacterial overgrowth, which produces endotoxin that burdens the liver, and the liver is where most T4-to-T3 conversion happens. This creates a self-reinforcing loop: poor thyroid slows the gut, and a compromised gut further suppresses thyroid function.

### A Dietary Gap Worth Exploring

Your eating pattern, while well-intentioned, may be working against your goals. Smoothies and soups are low in the caloric density and specific nutrients your metabolism depends on. You mentioned a plant-based approach, and while your dinners include meat, the overall picture suggests you may not be getting enough protein, saturated fat, or key micronutrients (B12, zinc, selenium, iodine, vitamin A as retinol) earlier in the day when your body needs fuel most. That early evening crash is telling: your body is running out of readily available energy well before the day is over. This pattern is consistent with under-fueling during the hours your metabolism should be most active.

### Brain Fog Has a Metabolic Signature

The mental clarity issues you're experiencing likely aren't random. When thyroid function is low, the brain is one of the first organs to feel it. Reduced T3 means less energy available to neurons. Add in the potential for gut-derived inflammation reaching the brain, and the cognitive effects compound. Your two primary goals, better gut health and sharper mental clarity, may share a single upstream cause.

---

Your symptoms aren't scattered. They form a coherent pattern that traces back to energy production, gut function, and the nutrient environment supporting both. A quiz can only capture so much, and there's more to explore beneath these patterns, but the connections here are strong and worth investigating further.
